question:
Given a finite string $S$ of symbols $X$ and $O$, we write $\Delta(S)$
for the number of $X$'s in $S$ minus the number of $O$'s. For example,
$\Delta(XOOXOOX) = -1$. We call a string $S$ \textbf{balanced} if every
substring $T$ of (consecutive symbols of) $S$ has $-2 \leq \Delta(T)
\leq 2$. Thus, $XOOXOOX$ is not balanced, since it contains the
substring $OOXOO$. Find, with proof, the number of balanced strings of
length $n$.
solution:
Consider a $1 \times n$ checkerboard, in which we write an $n$-letter
string, one letter per square. If the string is balanced, we can cover
each pair of adjacent squares containing the same letter with a $1
\times 2$ domino, and these will not overlap (because no three in a
row can be the same). Moreover, any domino is separated from the next
by an even number of squares, since they must cover opposite letters,
and the sequence must alternate in between.

Conversely, any arrangement of dominoes where adjacent dominoes are
separated by an even number of squares corresponds to a unique
balanced string, once we choose whether the string starts with $X$ or
$O$. In other words, the number of balanced strings is twice the
number of acceptable domino arrangements.

We count these arrangements by numbering the squares $0,1,\dots,n-1$
and distinguishing whether the dominoes start on even or odd numbers.
Once this is decided, one simply chooses whether or not to put a
domino in each eligible position. Thus
we have $2^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}$ arrangements in the first case and $2^{\lfloor
(n-1)/2 \rfloor}$ in the second, but note that the case of no dominoes has
been counted twice. Hence the number of balanced strings is
\[
2^{\lfloor (n+2)/2 \rfloor} + 2^{\lfloor (n+1)/2 \rfloor} - 2.
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Given a finite string $stringvar$ of symbols $exsymbol$ and $ohsymbol$, we write $diffcount(stringvar)$
for the number of $exsymbol$'s in $stringvar$ minus the number of $ohsymbol$'s. For example,
$diffcount(exsymbolohsymbolohsymbolexsymbolohsymbolohsymbolexsymbol) = -1$. We call a string $stringvar$ \textbf{balanced} if every
substring $substrg$ of (consecutive symbols of) $stringvar$ has $-2 \leq diffcount(substrg)
\leq 2$. Thus, $exsymbolohsymbolohsymbolexsymbolohsymbolohsymbolexsymbol$ is not balanced, since it contains the
substring $ohsymbolohsymbolexsymbolohsymbolohsymbol$. Find, with proof, the number of balanced strings of
length $lengthn$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Consider a $1 \times lengthn$ checkerboard, in which we write an $lengthn$-letter
string, one letter per square. If the string is balanced, we can cover
each pair of adjacent squares containing the same letter with a $1
\times 2$ domino, and these will not overlap (because no three in a
row can be the same). Moreover, any domino is separated from the next
by an even number of squares, since they must cover opposite letters,
and the sequence must alternate in between.

Conversely, any arrangement of dominoes where adjacent dominoes are
separated by an even number of squares corresponds to a unique
balanced string, once we choose whether the string starts with $exsymbol$ or
$ohsymbol$. In other words, the number of balanced strings is twice the
number of acceptable domino arrangements.

We count these arrangements by numbering the squares $0,1,\dots,lengthn-1$
and distinguishing whether the dominoes start on even or odd numbers.
Once this is decided, one simply chooses whether or not to put a
domino in each eligible position. Thus
we have $2^{\lfloor lengthn/2 \rfloor}$ arrangements in the first case and $2^{\lfloor
(lengthn-1)/2 \rfloor}$ in the second, but note that the case of no dominoes has
been counted twice. Hence the number of balanced strings is
\[
2^{\lfloor (lengthn+2)/2 \rfloor} + 2^{\lfloor (lengthn+1)/2 \rfloor} - 2.
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question: Given a finite string $lighthouse$ of symbols $porcupine$ and $dandelion$, we write $buttercup(lighthouse)$ for the number of $porcupine$'s in $lighthouse$ minus the number of $dandelion$'s. For example, $buttercup(porcupinedandeliondandelionporcupinedandeliondandelionporcupine) = -1$. We call a string $lighthouse$ \textbf{balanced} if every substring $watermelon$ of (consecutive symbols of) $lighthouse$ has $-2 \leq buttercup(watermelon) \leq 2$. Thus, $porcupinedandeliondandelionporcupinedandeliondandelionporcupine$ is not balanced, since it contains the substring $dandeliondandelionporcupinedandeliondandelion$. Find, with proof, the number of balanced strings of length $hieroglyph$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Consider a $1 \times hieroglyph$ checkerboard, in which we write an $hieroglyph$-letter string, one letter per square. If the string is balanced, we can cover each pair of adjacent squares containing the same letter with a $1 \times 2$ domino, and these will not overlap (because no three in a row can be the same). Moreover, any domino is separated from the next by an even number of squares, since they must cover opposite letters, and the sequence must alternate in between.

Conversely, any arrangement of dominoes where adjacent dominoes are separated by an even number of squares corresponds to a unique balanced string, once we choose whether the string starts with $porcupine$ or $dandelion$. In other words, the number of balanced strings is twice the number of acceptable domino arrangements.

We count these arrangements by numbering the squares $0,1,\dots,hieroglyph-1$ and distinguishing whether the dominoes start on even or odd numbers. Once this is decided, one simply chooses whether or not to put a domino in each eligible position. Thus
we have $2^{\lfloor hieroglyph/2 \rfloor}$ arrangements in the first case and $2^{\lfloor
(hieroglyph-1)/2 \rfloor}$ in the second, but note that the case of no dominoes has
been counted twice. Hence the number of balanced strings is
\[
2^{\lfloor (hieroglyph+2)/2 \rfloor} + 2^{\lfloor (hieroglyph+1)/2 \rfloor} - 2.
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question: Given a finite string $voidsequence$ of symbols $circlesymbol$ and $crosssymbol$, we write $equalityscore(voidsequence)$ for the number of $circlesymbol$'s in $voidsequence$ minus the number of $crosssymbol$'s. For example, $equalityscore(circlesymbolcrosssymbolcrosssymbolcirclesymbolcrosssymbolcrosssymbolcirclesymbol) = -1$. We call a string $voidsequence$ \textbf{balanced} if every substring $wholesequence$ of (consecutive symbols of) $voidsequence$ has $-2 \leq equalityscore(wholesequence) \leq 2$. Thus, $circlesymbolcrosssymbolcrosssymbolcirclesymbolcrosssymbolcrosssymbolcirclesymbol$ is not balanced, since it contains the substring $crosssymbolcrosssymbolcirclesymbolcrosssymbolcrosssymbol$. Find, with proof, the number of balanced strings of length $boundless$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Consider a $1 \times boundless$ checkerboard, in which we write an boundless-letter string, one letter per square. If the string is balanced, we can cover each pair of adjacent squares containing the same letter with a $1 \times 2$ domino, and these will not overlap (because no three in a row can be the same). Moreover, any domino is separated from the next by an even number of squares, since they must cover opposite letters, and the sequence must alternate in between.

Conversely, any arrangement of dominoes where adjacent dominoes are separated by an even number of squares corresponds to a unique balanced string, once we choose whether the string starts with $circlesymbol$ or $crosssymbol$. In other words, the number of balanced strings is twice the number of acceptable domino arrangements.

We count these arrangements by numbering the squares $0,1,\dots,boundless-1$ and distinguishing whether the dominoes start on even or odd numbers. Once this is decided, one simply chooses whether or not to put a domino in each eligible position. Thus
we have $2^{\lfloor boundless/2 \rfloor}$ arrangements in the first case and $2^{\lfloor
(boundless-1)/2 \rfloor}$ in the second, but note that the case of no dominoes has
been counted twice. Hence the number of balanced strings is
\[
2^{\lfloor (boundless+2)/2 \rfloor} + 2^{\lfloor (boundless+1)/2 \rfloor} - 2.
\]

garbled_string.question:
Given a finite string $kmrzclam$ of symbols $fxjwpldk$ and $zrmvchqa$, we write $ybqrstuv(kmrzclam)$
for the number of $fxjwpldk$'s in $kmrzclam$ minus the number of $zrmvchqa$'s. For example,
$ybqrstuv(fxjwpldkzrmvchqazrmvchqafxjwpldkzrmvchqazrmvchqafxjwpldk) = -1$. We call a string $kmrzclam$ \textbf{balanced} if every
substring $vaznikpo$ of (consecutive symbols of) $kmrzclam$ has $-2 \leq ybqrstuv(vaznikpo)
\leq 2$. Thus, $fxjwpldkzrmvchqazrmvchqafxjwpldkzrmvchqazrmvchqafxjwpldk$ is not balanced, since it contains the
substring $zrmvchqazrmvchqafxjwpldkzrmvchqazrmvchqa$. Find, with proof, the number of balanced strings of
length $qewrtuio$.
garbled_string.solution:
Consider a $1 \times qewrtuio$ checkerboard, in which we write an $qewrtuio$-letter
string, one letter per square. If the string is balanced, we can cover
each pair of adjacent squares containing the same letter with a $1
\times 2$ domino, and these will not overlap (because no three in a
row can be the same). Moreover, any domino is separated from the next
by an even number of squares, since they must cover opposite letters,
and the sequence must alternate in between.

Conversely, any arrangement of dominoes where adjacent dominoes are
separated by an even number of squares corresponds to a unique
balanced string, once we choose whether the string starts with $fxjwpldk$ or
$zrmvchqa$. In other words, the number of balanced strings is twice the
number of acceptable domino arrangements.

We count these arrangements by numbering the squares $0,1,\dots,qewrtuio-1$
and distinguishing whether the dominoes start on even or odd numbers.
Once this is decided, one simply chooses whether or not to put a
domino in each eligible position. Thus
we have $2^{\lfloor qewrtuio/2 \rfloor}$ arrangements in the first case and $2^{\lfloor
(qewrtuio-1)/2 \rfloor}$ in the second, but note that the case of no dominoes has
been counted twice. Hence the number of balanced strings is
\[
2^{\lfloor (qewrtuio+2)/2 \rfloor} + 2^{\lfloor (qewrtuio+1)/2 \rfloor} - 2.
\]

kernel_variant.question:
For every non-negative integer n consider words  
 W = w_1w_2\cdots w_n of length n over the alphabet  
 A = { N , S , E , W }.  

Associate to the four letters the planar unit steps  

 N \mapsto  (0,+1), S \mapsto  (0,-1), E \mapsto  (+1,0), W \mapsto  (-1,0).

Write  

 (x_t , y_t) = \Sigma _{i=1}^{t} w_i  (0 \leq  t \leq  n)

for the position of the walk after the first t steps and call the word W

 confined  if -2 \leq  x_t \leq  2 and -2 \leq  y_t \leq  2 for every t.

Let T_n denote the number of confined words of length n that start and finish at the origin (0,0).  
Determine T_n in closed form.
kernel_variant.solution:
1.  A graph-theoretic model.  
    The 25 lattice points (x,y) with -2 \leq  x,y \leq  2 form the vertex set V(G) of the Cartesian product graph  
  G = P_5 \square  P_5,  
    where P_5 is the path -2---1--0--1--2.  
    A letter of A moves the walker to one of the four adjacent vertices of G, hence a confined word of length n is precisely an n-step walk in G that starts and ends at the centre  

  c = (0,0).

    With A the 25 \times  25 adjacency matrix of G we therefore have  

  T_n = (A^n)_{c,c}.                                                              (1)

2.  Separating the two dimensions.  
    Label the vertices of P_5 by 1,\ldots ,5 corresponding to -2,\ldots ,2 and let A_1 be its 5 \times  5 adjacency matrix.  
    Because G = P_5 \square  P_5 one has  

  A = A_1 \otimes  I_5 + I_5 \otimes  A_1,

    the Kronecker sum of A_1 with itself.

    The path P_5 is well known to have eigen-values and an orthonormal eigen-basis  

  \lambda _r = 2 cos(r\pi /6),  u_{k,r} = \sqrt{2/6} sin(kr\pi /6),                   1 \leq  k,r \leq  5.   (2)

    (Here k indexes the vertex and r the eigen-value.)

    The centre of P_5 is the vertex k = 3, and the diagonal entry of a power of A_1 at this vertex reads  

  (A_1^n)_{3,3} = \Sigma _{r=1}^{5} u_{3,r}^2 \lambda _r^n
              = (2/6) \Sigma _{r=1}^{5} sin^2(3r\pi /6) \lambda _r^n.                     (3)

    Since sin(3r\pi /6)=0 for even r, only r = 1,3,5 contribute, giving

  (A_1^n)_{3,3} = (1/3)(\lambda _1^n + \lambda _3^n + \lambda _5^n).                                 (4)

    Numerical values: \lambda _1 = \sqrt{3}, \lambda _3 = 0, \lambda _5 = -\sqrt{3.}                        (5)

3.  Spectrum of the Cartesian product.  
   For a graph Cartesian product the spectrum adds: the 25 eigen-values of A are \lambda _r+\lambda _s (1 \leq  r,s \leq  5) with eigen-vectors u_{\cdot ,r} \otimes  u_{\cdot ,s}.  Consequently,

  (A^n)_{c,c}
  = \Sigma _{r,s=1}^{5} (u_{3,r}^2)(u_{3,s}^2) (\lambda _r+\lambda _s)^n
  = [(1/3)]^2 \Sigma _{r odd} \Sigma _{s odd} (\lambda _r+\lambda _s)^n    (by (4))              (6)

   because only r,s = 1,3,5 survive.  Using (5) we obtain the explicit nine summands

  T_n = (1/9){(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{3})^n+(\sqrt{3}+0)^n+(\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{3})^n
               + (0+\sqrt{3})^n+(0+0)^n+(0-\sqrt{3})^n
               + (-\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{3})^n+(-\sqrt{3}+0)^n+(-\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{3})^n}.                       (7)

   All terms containing a factor 0^n vanish for n\geq 1, and grouping the remaining four equal pairs gives

  T_n = (1/9)[(2\sqrt{3})^n + (-2\sqrt{3})^n + 2(\sqrt{3})^n + 2(-\sqrt{3})^n],  n\geq 1.             (8)

4.  Parity considerations.  
   The graph G is bipartite, hence no closed walk of odd length exists; equivalently, the right-hand side of (8) is 0 for odd n because the four non-zero summands cancel out.  Put n=2m (m\geq 0).  The signs disappear and

  T_{2m} = (1/9)[(2\sqrt{3})^{2m} + (-2\sqrt{3})^{2m} + 2(\sqrt{3})^{2m} + 2(-\sqrt{3})^{2m}]
         = (2/9)[(4\cdot 3)^{m} + 2\cdot 3^{m}]
         = (2/9)(12^{m} + 2\cdot 3^{m}).                                    (9)

5.  Initial value.  
   The empty word is confined, so T_0 = 1.

6.  Small-n check.  
   m = 1: T_2 = (2/9)(12+6)=4.  
   m = 2: T_4 = (2/9)(144+18)=36.  
   m = 3: T_6 = (2/9)(1728+54)=396.  
   These values agree with an exhaustive computer enumeration of all 4^{ n } words for n\leq 6.

Final closed form:

 T_0 = 1,  T_n = 0 for odd n,

 T_{2m} = (2/9)(12^{m} + 2\cdot 3^{m})   for m \geq  1.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension & more variables: the problem moves from one sign
  difference to simultaneous horizontal and vertical imbalances, and
  the state space grows from 2 kinds of letters to 4 and from 5 to 25
  possible running–sum states.  
• Sophisticated structures: the solution demands knowledge of Cartesian
  graph products, tensor products of matrices, and spectral graph
  theory; simple domino or colouring tricks no longer suffice.  
• Deeper theory: diagonalising the adjacency matrix of P₅, using
  orthonormal eigenbases, and exploiting the additive property of
  eigen-values in Cartesian products are all linear–algebraic tools
  well beyond the counting arguments of the original exercise.  
• More steps & interactions: one has to (i) recast the word problem as
  a walk on P₅□P₅; (ii) analyse one-dimensional spectra; (iii) lift the
  result to two dimensions via Kronecker products; (iv) re-assemble the
  data to obtain a closed formula, and (v) verify parity properties.  

These layers of algebraic and combinatorial reasoning make the enhanced
variant significantly harder than both the original problem and the
current kernel version.
